lesson.title: Solving Quadratics by Factoring
lesson.body: **Solving by factoring** uses the **Zero Product Property**: if A·B = 0, then A = 0 or B = 0. Steps: (1) Write in standard form ax²+bx+c=0. (2) Factor the left side. (3) Set each factor equal to zero. (4) Solve each linear equation. Example: x²+5x+6=0 → (x+2)(x+3)=0 → x=−2 or x=−3. Works when you can find integer factor pairs. If the quadratic doesn't factor nicely, use the quadratic formula instead.

rooms.question: Solve: x² − 16 = 0
rooms.explanation: Difference of squares: x²−16=(x−4)(x+4)=0. Roots: x=4 or x=−4. This can also be solved by square roots: x²=16 → x=±4. Both methods give the same answer.

rooms.question: Solve: x² + 10x + 25 = 0
rooms.explanation: Perfect square: (x+5)²=0 → x+5=0 → x=−5. This is a **double root** — the parabola just touches the x-axis at one point (tangent). The discriminant = 100−4(25) = 0, confirming exactly one root.

rooms.question: Order the steps to solve: 3x² − 12x = 0
items: Set each factor to zero: 3x = 0 or x − 4 = 0
rooms.explanation: When c=0, always factor out x (or GCF containing x) first. x=0 is always one root. Many students divide by x, which loses the x=0 solution — never divide by a variable.

boss.name: The Factoring Daemon
boss.taunt: Zero product property. One lethal outcome.

questions.question: Solve: x² = 3x + 18
questions.explanation: Standard form: x²−3x−18=0. Factor: product −18, sum −3: {−6,+3}. (x−6)(x+3)=0. x=6 or x=−3. Check: 36=18+18 ✓ and 9=−9+18 ✓.

questions.question: The product of two consecutive integers is 72. Find the integers.
questions.explanation: n(n+1)=72 → n²+n−72=0. Factor: product −72, sum 1: {9,−8}. (n+9)(n−8)=0 → n=−9 or n=8. If n=8: integers are 8 and 9. If n=−9: integers are −9 and −8. Both pairs have product 72. The problem has two valid solutions: {8,9} and {−9,−8}.
